category: Section 1: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture
question: Case Study: Cymbal Retail. Cymbal wants to modernize its operations by generating product attributes and images from supplier-provided information using Generative AI. They must incorporate a Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) review process before the live catalog (stored in Cloud SQL for MySQL) is updated. Which architecture provides the most scalable, cost-effective, and serverless approach while meeting these exact requirements?
options: Use Vertex AI Gemini for text and Imagen for image generation. Store intermediate results in Firestore. Deploy a Cloud Run web app for the HITL UI, which upon approval, triggers an Eventarc event to a Cloud Run function to update Cloud SQL.

Use the Cloud Vision API and Cloud Natural Language API. Store the generated content in Cloud Storage. Deploy an App Engine standard app for associates to review content, writing approved changes via Datastream to Cloud SQL.

Run open-source LLMs on a GKE Standard cluster with attached GPUs. Store intermediate generated assets locally on Persistent Disks. Expose a Kubernetes service for associates to review and directly update Cloud SQL via JDBC.

Ingest supplier data into BigQuery. Use BigQuery ML to generate text attributes and call Vertex AI Imagen remotely. Use Looker as the HITL UI to review data, and scheduled queries to push approved data to Cloud SQL.
explanation: Vertex AI provides the foundational models (Gemini for text, Imagen for images) needed. Storing intermediate, unapproved state in a fast NoSQL DB like Firestore is highly efficient. Cloud Run offers a scalable, serverless platform for the custom HITL UI. Once approved, using event-driven architectures (Eventarc + Cloud Run functions) to update the relational Cloud SQL database ensures decoupling, high scalability, and minimal operational overhead.

category: Section 1: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture
question: Case Study: KnightMotives Automotive. KnightMotives plans to monetize the vast amounts of telemetry data generated by its autonomous vehicle fleet to finance new technology investments. They need to securely ingest this data from global locations (including rural areas with intermittent connectivity), process it in real-time, and expose specific aggregated datasets to external automotive partners without moving the data. Which combination of services should they implement? (Select 2)
options: Use Cloud IoT Core with MQTT protocol to buffer data locally on the vehicles during rural disconnects, securely transmitting to Cloud Pub/Sub upon reconnection.
Deploy a global external Application Load Balancer in front of Cloud Run services to act as an ingestion gateway, writing validated payloads directly to Cloud Storage.
Publish the aggregated datasets to BigQuery and securely share them with external partners using Analytics Hub, granting subscribe access to authorized external identities.
Use Pub/Sub to ingest the telemetry data stream and Dataflow to process and clean the data in real-time before writing the aggregated results to BigQuery.
Configure VPC Service Controls perimeters around BigQuery and export the aggregated data via Cloud Storage signed URLs to external partners.
explanation: Pub/Sub is the standard Google Cloud entry point for high-throughput, globally distributed telemetry streaming, and Dataflow handles real-time stream processing and aggregation into BigQuery. To monetize and share this data securely with external partners without duplicating or moving the data, BigQuery Analytics Hub is the precise enterprise solution. Cloud IoT Core is deprecated and no longer an active Google-recommended pattern.

category: Section 1: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture
question: Guided by the Performance Optimization pillar of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework, your enterprise is migrating an intensive high-performance computing (HPC) application. The workload consists of tightly coupled MPI (Message Passing Interface) nodes that require single-digit microsecond latency between instances. The job runs predictably at the end of each month for exactly 48 hours. Which compute deployment strategy guarantees the necessary network performance while optimizing costs?
options: Deploy the nodes on GKE Autopilot in a single region to ensure dynamic scaling, utilizing Spot Pods to minimize the 48-hour compute costs.
Deploy Compute Engine C2 (Compute-optimized) instances within a single zone using a Compact Placement Policy, and leverage 1-year Committed Use Discounts (CUDs).
Deploy Compute Engine instances using a Spread Placement Policy across multiple zones within the same region to balance high availability and low latency.
Deploy Compute Engine C2 (Compute-optimized) instances within a single zone using a Compact Placement Policy, and provision them on-demand without CUDs.
explanation: For tightly coupled HPC workloads requiring microsecond latency, instances must be placed as physically close to each other as possible. A Compact Placement Policy in a single zone guarantees this proximity. Compute-optimized (C2) machine types are specifically designed for HPC. Because the workload only runs 48 hours a month, purchasing a 1-year or 3-year Committed Use Discount (CUD) would be highly cost-inefficient; paying on-demand for those 48 hours is vastly cheaper.

category: Section 2: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure
question: Your organization is provisioning a multi-cloud network architecture linking Google Cloud with AWS. You need to establish highly available, secure connectivity with a guaranteed throughput of 2 Gbps. You want to minimize hardware procurement lead times and physical cross-connect management overhead. Which provisioning approach best satisfies these requirements?
options: Provision a Dedicated Interconnect circuit using a colocation facility, configuring BGP over two 10 Gbps physical links.
Deploy a Partner Interconnect connection through a supported service provider, provisioning two 1 Gbps VLAN attachments for high availability.
Configure an HA VPN over the public internet between Google Cloud Router and AWS Transit Gateway, utilizing multiple aggregated tunnels to achieve the 2 Gbps throughput.
Use Cross-Cloud Network with Network Connectivity Center to automatically provision private fiber lines between the cloud providers without third-party ISPs.
explanation: HA VPN provides a highly available (99.99% SLA), secure IPsec connection over the public internet. Since the bandwidth requirement is 2 Gbps, you can easily achieve this by aggregating multiple VPN tunnels (each tunnel supports up to 3 Gbps). This approach avoids the significant physical overhead, lead time, and long-term contracts associated with Dedicated or Partner Interconnect, which are generally meant for much higher throughputs or strict on-premise physical compliance needs.

category: Section 2: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure
question: A retail client is managing their expanding global inventory database on Cloud Spanner. The database has grown to 50 TB, but they are observing severe read latency hotspots during flash sales when millions of users repeatedly query the exact same top 10 trending items simultaneously. Which two architectural provisioning strategies should you apply to alleviate these hotspots while maintaining horizontal scalability? (Select 2)
options: Implement a Memorystore for Redis cache cluster in front of Cloud Spanner to absorb high-frequency read requests for the trending items.
Redesign the Spanner schema to monotonically increase the primary keys of the products table to ensure sequential data distribution.
Use a Spanner bit-reverse sequence or UUID (Universally Unique Identifier) for the primary key of the products table to prevent write hotspots.
Provision a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL read replica in each region to offload the analytical read queries from the primary Spanner instances.
Utilize Cloud Spanner's built-in query statistics and Key Visualizer to continuously monitor and pinpoint the exact row keys causing the read hotspots.
explanation: Read hotspots occur when a massive volume of traffic targets a specific row or narrow key range (like the top 10 trending items). A caching layer like Memorystore for Redis is the standard architecture to absorb massive read spikes on a tiny subset of data. Using Key Visualizer allows the team to visually monitor access patterns and definitively identify these hotspots. Monotonically increasing keys actually *cause* hotspots. UUIDs fix *write* hotspots but do not solve single-row *read* hotspots for popular items.

category: Section 3: Designing for security and compliance
question: Case Study: EHR Healthcare. EHR Healthcare hosts highly sensitive Electronic Health Records (EHR) on GKE. To maintain stringent regulatory compliance (HIPAA), they must ensure that pods processing patient records cannot make unauthorized outbound connections to the public internet, while still allowing egress to specific managed Google APIs (e.g., Cloud Storage) without routing over the public internet. How should you design this security control?
options: Deploy a NAT Gateway on the VPC and configure Kubernetes egress Network Policies to deny all internet-bound traffic except to the NAT IP.
Configure Private Google Access on the VPC subnet housing the GKE cluster, and implement Kubernetes Network Policies to block all pod egress traffic except to the private.googleapis.com IP range.
Enable VPC Service Controls to create a service perimeter around the GKE cluster, and configure Cloud Armor egress rules to filter malicious outbound requests.
Assign public IP addresses to the GKE nodes but use Google Cloud Next Generation Firewall (NGFW) policies to drop all packets with an external destination.
explanation: Private Google Access allows resources without external IP addresses (like nodes in a private GKE cluster) to reach Google APIs and services using Google's internal network infrastructure. By coupling this with Kubernetes Network Policies that explicitly drop all egress traffic *except* to the IP range used by Private Google Access (`private.googleapis.com` VIPs), you achieve the strict isolation required by HIPAA while seamlessly allowing integration with essential GCP services.

category: Section 3: Designing for security and compliance
question: Based on the 'Implement Zero Trust' and 'Least Privilege' principles of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework, your security team needs to audit and restrict elevated access in your production environment. Developers currently have persistent `roles/editor` access to troubleshoot live issues, leading to compliance violations. Which combination of actions should you take to design a secure, compliant access model? (Select 2)
options: Revoke the persistent `roles/editor` access and implement Privileged Access Manager (PAM) to grant just-in-time, time-bound elevated access based on manual approvals.
Create a custom IAM role that duplicates the exact permissions of `roles/editor` but restrict its usage geographically using VPC Service Controls.
Utilize IAM Recommender (Policy Intelligence) to analyze access patterns over the last 90 days and apply the auto-generated least-privilege custom roles.
Grant developers the `roles/viewer` role globally and require them to SSH into production Compute Engine VMs to execute write operations via local service accounts.
Implement Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) on the Google Cloud Console to restrict access strictly to users within the corporate office network.
explanation: To enforce least privilege and zero trust, developers should not have persistent broad access (like Editor). IAM Recommender analyzes actual usage to suggest minimal roles tailored to developer activity. For necessary break-glass or elevated troubleshooting scenarios, Privileged Access Manager (PAM) provides just-in-time, auditable, and time-bound access. Using custom roles to duplicate Editor permissions or bypassing IAM via SSH violates compliance and auditing best practices.

category: Section 3: Designing for security and compliance
question: A government agency mandates that all highly sensitive data processed in Google Cloud must be encrypted at rest using keys that the agency retains ultimate physical control over within their own on-premises Hardware Security Modules (HSMs). They need to use these keys seamlessly with BigQuery for analytics. Which cryptographic architecture meets this regulatory need?
options: Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) via Cloud KMS backed by a Google Cloud HSM.
Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK) dynamically provided in API requests to BigQuery.
Cloud External Key Manager (Cloud EKM) configured to communicate over a secure connection to the agency's on-premises HSM.
Default Google-managed encryption keys, supplemented with client-side application encryption before loading data into BigQuery.
explanation: Cloud External Key Manager (Cloud EKM) allows organizations to use keys stored in an external key management system (like an on-prem HSM) to protect data at rest in supported Google Cloud services, including BigQuery. This ensures the agency retains ultimate physical control over the keys. Cloud KMS HSM uses Google's hardware. CSEK does not seamlessly integrate with BigQuery table encryption in the same way, and client-side encryption breaks BigQuery's native analytical capabilities.

category: Section 4: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes
question: Case Study: Altostrat Media. Altostrat’s operations team complains of alert fatigue. Their current open-source monitoring systems generate hundreds of email notifications daily for minor CPU spikes during scheduled video transcoding jobs, causing critical database failures to go unnoticed. To optimize this technical process and align with SRE best practices, what should they do?
options: Write a script to automatically delete all alerts that originate from the video transcoding instances to reduce inbox clutter.

Define Service Level Objectives (SLOs) tied to user journeys, configure alerts to trigger only when the Error Budget burn rate exceeds a critical threshold, and route them to an incident management platform.
Migrate from Prometheus to Cloud Monitoring, and lower the CPU utilization alert thresholds so they only trigger when an instance hits 100% capacity for 1 hour.
Hire a dedicated Tier 1 support team to manually review all emails and escalate only the critical database alerts to the core SRE team.
explanation: A core SRE practice to combat alert fatigue is to alert on symptoms that impact the user, not isolated causes (like temporary CPU spikes). By defining SLOs (e.g., successful transcoding jobs within a timeframe) and alerting on the Error Budget burn rate, the team is only notified when a systemic issue genuinely threatens the business agreement, allowing them to ignore transient noise and focus on critical failures.

category: Section 4: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes
question: Following the Cost Optimization pillar of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework, your enterprise is analyzing their monthly BigQuery spend. They notice costs have skyrocketed due to a mix of automated dashboard queries and unpredictable ad-hoc queries from data scientists analyzing petabytes of raw data. Which combination of optimizations should you apply to analyze and reduce these business costs? (Select 2)
options: Transition the predictable automated dashboard workloads to BigQuery Editions (capacity-based pricing) to establish a baseline cost structure.
Enforce a BigQuery custom quota setting a maximum bytes billed per query specifically for the data scientists' projects.
Convert all raw data tables to JSON format to compress storage sizes, drastically reducing the cost of full table scans.
Disable BigQuery result caching, as storing cached results incurs high regional persistent disk storage fees.
Move all data scientists' workloads to Cloud SQL, as relational databases inherently prevent large analytical table scans.
explanation: BigQuery Editions (capacity/flat-rate pricing) is ideal for predictable, steady-state workloads like automated dashboards, providing cost certainty and reducing on-demand spikes. For unpredictable ad-hoc queries, setting a custom quota for 'maximum bytes billed per query' serves as a technical guardrail, preventing a single poorly written query (e.g., `SELECT *` on a petabyte table) from causing massive billing spikes. Caching in BigQuery is actually free and drastically reduces query costs.

category: Section 5: Managing implementation
question: Your team is managing the deployment of a microservices application using Terraform. To comply with auditing policies, the security team needs an automated way to verify that no infrastructure drift has occurred (i.e., manual changes made via the Cloud Console overriding the Terraform configuration) and automatically notify the security Slack channel if drift is detected. How should you manage this implementation process?
options: Configure VPC Flow Logs to monitor all API calls made to the cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com endpoint and filter for manual edits.
Implement a scheduled CI/CD pipeline using Cloud Build that runs `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode`. Use a Cloud Run function to send the results to Slack if the exit code is non-zero.
Use the Cloud Asset Inventory API to export the state to BigQuery every hour and compare the hash with the Terraform terraform.tfstate file stored in Cloud Storage.
Set up an Organization Policy constraint that strictly denies any user access to the Google Cloud Console UI, forcing all interactions through the CLI.
explanation: Running `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode` checks the real-world infrastructure against the defined configuration. If drift exists, the command exits with a specific non-zero code. Scheduling this in a CI/CD pipeline (like Cloud Build) and triggering a notification via Pub/Sub and Cloud Run functions is the industry-standard way to monitor and manage infrastructure-as-code drift.

category: Section 5: Managing implementation
question: Case Study: KnightMotives Automotive. KnightMotives is heavily fragmented with multiple codebases across its vehicle lines. To modernize their IT infrastructure, they are migrating to Google Cloud and want to establish a robust API management platform to safely expose their legacy ERP backend systems to modern mobile applications and third-party dealer portals. Which implementation steps are necessary using Apigee? (Select 2)
options: Deploy an Apigee API proxy to decouple the modern mobile applications from the backend legacy ERP systems.
Configure Apigee to route all API calls over the public internet directly to the on-premises mainframe to avoid VPN tunneling costs.
Implement OAuth 2.0 and API key verification policies within Apigee to secure access for third-party dealer portals.
Use API Gateway instead of Apigee, as Apigee is not designed for hybrid architectures involving on-premises mainframes.
Refactor the entire legacy ERP system into microservices before attempting to configure Apigee routing policies.
explanation: Apigee is Google Cloud's premier enterprise API management platform. Deploying API proxies acts as a facade, successfully decoupling modern frontend clients from legacy backend systems without requiring immediate, high-risk refactoring. Apigee provides robust built-in policies to enforce security, such as OAuth 2.0 and API keys, strictly managing who (e.g., external dealers) can securely access the internal ERP data.

category: Section 6: Ensuring solution and operations reliability
question: You have deployed a critical, globally distributed web application on Compute Engine Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) behind a Global External Application Load Balancer. To ensure operations reliability, you want the load balancer to automatically route traffic to a custom static 'Maintenance' page hosted on Cloud Storage if all backend MIGs fail their health checks simultaneously. How should you ensure this solution reliability?
options: Set up a Cloud DNS routing policy with a health check that redirects to the Cloud Storage bucket URL on failure.
Configure custom error handling within the backend MIG template to serve a local HTML file if the application crashes.
Add a backend bucket containing the maintenance page to the existing URL map, and configure a custom error response policy on the load balancer to route 5xx errors to this backend bucket.
Use Cloud Armor to detect backend timeouts and inject a custom HTML response payload using an edge security policy.
explanation: Global External Application Load Balancers natively support custom error responses. You can configure the load balancer to intercept specific HTTP status codes (like 502 Bad Gateway if backends are unhealthy) and return a custom error page stored in an attached backend bucket (Cloud Storage). This ensures users see a friendly, branded maintenance page rather than a raw browser error, maintaining operational trust.

category: Section 6: Ensuring solution and operations reliability
question: Guided by the Reliability pillar of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework, your organization is designing a disaster recovery strategy for a mission-critical transactional workload on Cloud SQL. The business demands an RPO (Recovery Point Objective) of zero and an RTO (Recovery Time Objective) of under 2 minutes for a zonal failure within the primary region. What architecture pattern must you deploy?
options: A Cloud SQL instance configured with automated daily backups and point-in-time recovery (PITR) enabled.
A Cloud SQL instance with Regional High Availability (HA) enabled, providing synchronous replication to a standby instance in a different zone.
A single Cloud SQL instance paired with Datastream to replicate data asynchronously to a separate disaster recovery region.
A Cloud SQL instance configured with an external read replica in a different region, triggering a manual promotion via Cloud Run functions upon failure.
explanation: Regional High Availability (HA) in Cloud SQL provides synchronous replication between a primary instance and a standby instance located in different zones within the same region. This synchronously guarantees an RPO of 0 (no committed transactions are lost) and an RTO of typically 1-2 minutes (the time it takes for the automatic failover to detect the issue and promote the standby). Cross-region replicas rely on asynchronous replication, which cannot guarantee an exact RPO of 0.

category: Section 2: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure
question: Case Study: Cymbal Retail. Cymbal Retail relies on a hybrid environment with massive volumes of daily product inventory updates originating from an on-premises Microsoft SQL Server. They want to modernize their stack by streaming these updates continuously into BigQuery to power real-time dashboards for their Conversational Commerce agents, without writing custom extract/load code. Which combination of services provisions this pipeline? (Select 2)
options: Provision a Datastream connection to capture Change Data Capture (CDC) events directly from the on-premises Microsoft SQL Server.
Deploy Cloud Data Fusion to execute hourly batch scripts via a JDBC driver to poll the on-premises database for changes.
Configure Datastream with a native BigQuery destination to seamlessly replicate the CDC events into BigQuery tables.
Deploy a custom Dataflow pipeline utilizing the Apache Beam JDBC IO connector to continuously query the database and stream into BigQuery.
Use Dataflow with a Google-provided template to read the Datastream CDC stream from Cloud Storage and write it to BigQuery.
explanation: Datastream is Google Cloud's serverless Change Data Capture (CDC) and replication service, natively supporting source databases like Microsoft SQL Server. To seamlessly load this data into BigQuery without writing custom Dataflow code or heavy ETL pipelines, you can directly configure Datastream with a native BigQuery destination, which continuously replicates the CDC events into analytics-ready tables.

category: Section 1: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture
question: Your company operates a multicloud environment spanning Google Cloud and AWS. You need to deploy a highly available, high-bandwidth (10 Gbps) private connection between the two cloud providers without routing traffic over the public internet or utilizing a third-party colocation facility. Which Google Cloud networking solution directly fulfills this architecture requirement?
options: Cross-Cloud Interconnect.
Dedicated Interconnect.
HA VPN over Cloud Router.
Partner Interconnect via a supported ISP.
explanation: Cross-Cloud Interconnect enables organizations to establish high-bandwidth (10 Gbps or 100 Gbps), dedicated private connections directly between Google Cloud and other supported cloud providers (like AWS, Azure) without requiring a physical footprint in a colocation facility or routing sensitive traffic over the public internet.

category: Section 2: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure
question: You are setting up a secure continuous integration (CI) pipeline using Cloud Build. The build process must access internal source code repositories hosted on-premises via Cloud VPN, and the build workers must not have external public IP addresses. Which configurations are required? (Select 2)
options: Configure Cloud Build to use private pools assigned to a VPC network peered with the on-premises network.
Enable the Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) integration and run the Cloud Build workers as custom pods within a private cluster.
Disable the default global public pool execution by setting an Organization Policy constraint.
Assign external IP addresses temporarily during the build phase via a Cloud Nat gateway, then immediately destroy them.
Route all Cloud Build traffic through a Cloud Armor security policy designed to drop external ingress.
explanation: To access resources on a private network (like an on-premises network via VPN) and ensure workers do not have external public IPs, Cloud Build private pools must be used and peered directly to your VPC. To strictly enforce this security posture and prevent developers from accidentally using the public shared pool, you must implement the 'constraints/cloudbuild.allowedWorkerPools' Organization Policy constraint.

category: Section 3: Designing for security and compliance
question: Case Study: EHR Healthcare. EHR is migrating massive volumes of legacy relational databases to Cloud SQL and BigQuery. Before the migration begins, the compliance team requires an automated way to discover, classify, and report on the presence of unencrypted Patient Health Information (PHI) across their entire organizational footprint in Google Cloud. What is the most efficient technical approach?
options: Enable Sensitive Data Protection (DLP) discovery at the organization or folder level to automatically profile data across BigQuery, Cloud SQL, and Cloud Storage.
Export all data to Cloud Storage and configure a Cloud Run function to scan every object using the Cloud Natural Language API.
Provision a Dataproc cluster running Apache Spark to crawl all database tables nightly and execute regex pattern matching for PHI formats.
Use Security Command Center Premium to run Container Threat Detection on the databases to flag plaintext PHI.
explanation: Sensitive Data Protection (formerly Cloud DLP) provides automated, organization-wide discovery and profiling capabilities. By enabling it at the org or folder level, it continuously scans and creates security data profiles across BigQuery, Cloud SQL, and Cloud Storage without requiring custom data pipelines, expensive exports, or complex regex scripts.

category: Section 4: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes
question: Following the Reliability pillar of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework, your e-commerce application is facing unprecedented traffic during a major holiday sale. The product recommendation microservice is severely overloaded and crashing, threatening to bring down the entire checkout pipeline. What architectural pattern should you implement to analyze and mitigate this process bottleneck?
options: Implement graceful degradation by catching timeouts from the recommendation service and returning a default, static list of popular products to the user instead of failing the request.
Configure the recommendation service to retry failing database queries indefinitely until a successful response is received.
Use Cloud Load Balancing to immediately terminate the user sessions of clients originating from high-latency geographical regions.
Provision a massive Redis cache and load the entire product database into memory, disabling the primary recommendation engine completely.
explanation: Graceful degradation is a core cloud reliability pattern. Instead of allowing a non-critical feature (like personalized recommendations) to cause a hard failure for the entire page or critical path (checkout), the system catches the error/timeout and falls back to a simpler, less resource-intensive alternative (like a static list). Retrying indefinitely causes cascading resource exhaustion.

category: Section 5: Managing implementation
question: An enterprise operates dozens of Kubernetes clusters across Google Cloud, on-premises data centers, and AWS. They need to manage configuration consistently across all environments, enforcing RBAC and security policies dynamically without manually applying manifests to individual clusters. Which components of GKE Enterprise manage this implementation? (Select 2)
options: Connect all clusters to a unified GKE Fleet.
Use Config Sync to apply and continuously reconcile configurations from a central Git repository to all fleet clusters.
Deploy Cloud Service Mesh gateways explicitly on the on-premises hardware load balancers.
Utilize Google Cloud Deploy to push bash scripts executing `kubectl apply` iteratively across the multicloud nodes.
Install the Binary Authorization admission controller directly onto the AWS EKS nodes using a custom Helm chart.
explanation: A GKE Fleet is a logical grouping of Kubernetes clusters that can be managed together, establishing a boundary for policy and configuration regardless of the underlying cloud provider. Config Sync, a core component of GKE Enterprise, utilizes a GitOps approach to continuously apply, manage, and reconcile RBAC, security policies, and configurations across all clusters in the fleet from a centralized Git repository.

category: Section 1: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture
question: Case Study: EHR Healthcare. EHR Healthcare needs to securely share anonymized, aggregated patient trend data with third-party medical researchers globally. The researchers use various analytical tools natively within Google Cloud. EHR wants to manage access centrally, revoke access instantly if needed, and strictly avoid the costs and risks of duplicating data across projects. How should this architecture be designed?
options: Export the data to Cloud Storage in Parquet format and generate cryptographically signed URLs with strict expiration times.
Use BigQuery Analytics Hub to create an exchange and publish a shared dataset, granting the researchers subscriber access.
Create a VPC Service Controls perimeter around the EHR data warehouse and configure an ingress bridge to the researchers' projects.
Provision a Datastream pipeline to replicate the required rows directly into the researchers' individual Cloud SQL databases.
explanation: BigQuery Analytics Hub is purpose-built for secure, cross-organizational data sharing without requiring data movement or duplication. It allows the publisher to maintain a single source of truth, enforce strict access controls, and easily revoke access, perfectly satisfying EHR's need to share data securely and cost-effectively.

category: Section 6: Ensuring solution and operations reliability
question: Following the Reliability pillar of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework, your e-commerce application requires mechanisms to survive severe traffic spikes during flash sales without suffering cascading failures. The backend relies on a Compute Engine Managed Instance Group (MIG) that occasionally hits its absolute scaling limits. Which two architectural patterns should you implement to protect the backend? (Select 2)
options: Deploy Cloud Tasks to decouple and buffer asynchronous requests, smoothing out traffic spikes before they reach the backend.
Implement synchronous retries on the frontend with no exponential backoff to ensure requests eventually succeed.
Utilize Cloud Service Mesh to implement a Circuit Breaker pattern that fast-fails requests when the backend is overwhelmed.
Decrease the health check interval on the load balancer to 1 second to detect and remove overwhelmed nodes faster.
Replace the autoscaling MIG with a single, statically provisioned Compute-optimized (C2) virtual machine.
explanation: To prevent cascading failures during extreme load, buffering asynchronous workloads via a message queue (Cloud Tasks or Pub/Sub) absorbs the shock of sudden spikes. Additionally, implementing the Circuit Breaker pattern (via Cloud Service Mesh) detects when the backend is struggling and deliberately fast-fails incoming requests, preventing the exhaustion of threads and resources across the entire system.

category: Section 2: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure
question: You are tasked with provisioning a new Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) environment for a data science team. The team runs thousands of ad-hoc batch jobs daily, each with vastly different CPU and memory requirements. You need to eliminate cluster management overhead, strictly pay only for the resources the pods use, and avoid manual node pool configuration entirely. Which compute provisioning strategy should you choose?
options: Provision a GKE Standard cluster and enable the Kubernetes Cluster Autoscaler with a wide range of node sizes.
Provision a GKE Autopilot cluster, allowing Google to manage node provisioning and scaling based dynamically on pod specifications.
Deploy a fleet of Compute Engine Spot VMs managed by a Managed Instance Group (MIG) running custom Docker containers.
Utilize Cloud Run functions configured with maximum memory allocation to execute the batch jobs asynchronously.
explanation: GKE Autopilot is a fully managed operational mode that eliminates node management. Google automatically provisions, scales, and secures the underlying infrastructure based on the pod manifests. Crucially, billing in Autopilot is based purely on the vCPU, memory, and storage requested by the scheduled pods, preventing paying for unallocated overhead in the cluster.

category: Section 3: Designing for security and compliance
question: A financial enterprise requires that developers must be able to query sensitive financial data in BigQuery from their designated Compute Engine workstations to build ML models. However, strict data exfiltration policies dictate that developers must be entirely blocked from downloading or copying this data to their local laptops or external internet services. What is the most effective security configuration?
options: Remove the BigQuery Data Viewer role from the developers' IAM accounts, granting them only BigQuery Job User.
Create a VPC Service Controls perimeter encompassing the BigQuery API and the VPC hosting the developer workstations, disabling internet egress.
Enable Cloud Sensitive Data Protection (DLP) to automatically mask all numeric data within the BigQuery tables.
Configure Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) to enforce multi-factor authentication before developers can access the Google Cloud Console.
explanation: VPC Service Controls mitigates data exfiltration risks by creating a logical security perimeter around Google Cloud resources. By placing the developer VMs and the BigQuery API inside the same perimeter and blocking external egress, developers can seamlessly query the data for model training within the boundary, but are structurally prevented from exporting the data to outside networks or local machines.

category: Section 4: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes
question: Case Study: Altostrat Media. Altostrat Media is trying to optimize their massive Cloud Storage costs. They currently store 50 PB of raw video footage in the Standard storage class. Analysis shows that videos are actively accessed daily during the first 30 days of editing, occasionally accessed between 30 and 90 days, and virtually never accessed after 90 days, though they must be retained indefinitely for compliance. Which two configurations optimize this technical process? (Select 2)
options: Configure an Object Lifecycle Management rule to transition objects to Nearline storage after 30 days.
Configure an Object Lifecycle Management rule to transition objects to Archive storage after 90 days.
Export all data older than 90 days to a local SAN using Storage Transfer Service to avoid cloud costs.
Enable Cloud Storage Autoclass on the bucket to automatically compress the video files.
Set an Object Lifecycle Management rule to delete the objects after 90 days and rely on local backups.
explanation: Based on the access patterns, transitioning to Nearline after 30 days accommodates the "occasional access" phase cost-effectively. Transitioning to Archive after 90 days perfectly aligns with the "virtually never accessed but must be retained" compliance requirement, slashing storage costs significantly. Autoclass transitions classes but does not compress data. Deletion violates the indefinite retention requirement.

category: Section 3: Designing for security and compliance
question: Following the 'Implement shift-left security' principle from the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework, your organization mandates that no container image containing critical OS vulnerabilities can be deployed to your production GKE clusters. You use Cloud Build and Artifact Registry. What is the most robust, automated architecture to enforce this?
options: Use Artifact Analysis to scan images, and configure Binary Authorization with an attestor that requires a 'no critical vulnerabilities' cryptographic signature before scheduling.
Run a cron job that checks Artifact Registry every hour using a Python script, automatically deleting images flagged with critical CVEs.
Write a Cloud Run function that intercepts Kubernetes deployment manifests and checks the image tag against a hardcoded whitelist in Cloud Storage.
Rely on GKE's default ValidatingAdmissionWebhook to automatically block the deployment of images that are larger than 500 MB.
explanation: Binary Authorization is a deploy-time security control that ensures only trusted images run on GKE. By integrating with vulnerability scanning (Artifact Analysis) and requiring a specific attestation (cryptographic signature) verifying the scan passed without critical vulnerabilities, you enforce a strict, automated shift-left security gate directly at the cluster API level.

category: Section 5: Managing implementation
question: You are managing the implementation of a new inventory application deployed on Compute Engine. The application must interact securely with Cloud Storage and Pub/Sub APIs. Historically, developers have generated long-lived JSON service account keys and committed them to the application's source code repository. How should you secure this implementation to meet Google Cloud best practices?
options: Encrypt the JSON keys in the Git repository using Cloud Key Management Service (KMS) symmetric keys.
Delete the JSON keys entirely and assign a dedicated Service Account directly to the Compute Engine instances.
Move the JSON keys into Secret Manager and hardcode the Secret Manager resource paths in the application's configuration files.
Restrict the Git repository's IAM access to only Senior Developers and Security Administrators.
explanation: The primary security best practice for managing credentials on Google Cloud compute resources (like VMs, GKE, Cloud Run) is to attach a Service Account directly to the resource. The Google Cloud Client Libraries automatically fetch short-lived credentials via the metadata server, entirely eliminating the catastrophic risk of managing, rotating, or leaking long-lived JSON keys.

category: Section 2: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure
question: Case Study: Cymbal Retail. Cymbal Retail is provisioning a new multi-region architecture to handle its expanding global catalog. They require a fully managed database providing strong global consistency, ACID transactions, and a relational SQL interface. Furthermore, they need to stream all catalog modifications in real-time to an external analytics pipeline without placing heavy read locks on the primary database. Which two Google Cloud services must they provision? (Select 2)
options: Cloud Spanner.
Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL.
Cloud Spanner Change Streams processed via Cloud Dataflow.
BigQuery Data Transfer Service configured for hourly batched loads.
A Cloud SQL Read Replica deployed in the secondary region.
explanation: Cloud Spanner is the only fully managed relational database on Google Cloud that offers strong global consistency and ACID transactions across multiple continents. To stream real-time catalog modifications (CDC) without impacting primary database performance, Cloud Spanner Change Streams natively captures these events, which are typically processed and routed using Cloud Dataflow.

category: Section 4: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes
question: Following the Cost Optimization pillar of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework, you are auditing a legacy project. You discover hundreds of orphaned Persistent Disks (PDs) that were left behind when developers deleted their test VMs. You want to automate the identification and deletion of these unattached disks to halt ongoing storage charges. What is the recommended technical process?
options: Use the Active Assist Idle Resource Recommender API to identify unattached disks, and trigger a Cloud Run function to apply the deletion recommendation.
Set a Cloud Storage lifecycle policy on the Persistent Disks to auto-delete after 7 days of remaining unattached.
Export Cloud Asset Inventory data to BigQuery daily, and write a SQL query to drop the rows representing the orphaned disks.
Create an Organization Policy that prevents developers from deleting a VM if it still has a Persistent Disk attached.
explanation: Active Assist (specifically the Idle Resource Recommender) uses machine learning to automatically identify unattached/idle persistent disks. You can programmatically interact with the Recommender API (e.g., using Cloud Scheduler and Cloud Run functions) to automatically apply these recommendations (deleting or snapshotting the disks), ensuring continuous, hands-off cost optimization. PDs do not natively support lifecycle rules like GCS.

category: Section 1: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture
question: A financial institution is designing a data lakehouse. They possess petabytes of historical trading data residing in Cloud Storage in Parquet format. Business analysts need to query this data via BigQuery using standard SQL. However, a strict organizational policy forbids duplicating or moving the data into BigQuery's native storage layer, while concurrently mandating strict row-level and column-level security. How should you design the data access layer?
options: Create BigLake external tables over the Cloud Storage data, enabling BigQuery querying combined with fine-grained access control.
Create standard BigQuery external tables over the Cloud Storage data and use IAM conditions on the bucket.
Provision a Dataproc cluster to read the data on-demand and write temporary tables into BigQuery.
Load the data into a highly available Cloud SQL instance and utilize BigQuery federated queries.
explanation: BigLake tables unify data lakes and warehouses. They allow you to query structured data (like Parquet) directly in Cloud Storage using BigQuery without data duplication. Crucially, unlike standard external tables, BigLake tables enforce BigQuery's fine-grained security policies (row-level and column-level access controls) at the data lake storage level.

category: Section 3: Designing for security and compliance
question: Case Study: KnightMotives Automotive. KnightMotives needs to securely collect real-time telemetry data from millions of vehicles globally. The vehicles authenticate using X.509 certificates. Because Google Cloud IoT Core has been retired, KnightMotives requires a scalable, cloud-native architecture to ingest this MQTT traffic securely and route it to Google Cloud data pipelines. What is the recommended Google Cloud architecture pattern?
options: Deploy an enterprise third-party MQTT broker (e.g., EMQX, HiveMQ) on GKE or Compute Engine, configured to forward authenticated messages natively into Pub/Sub.
Reconfigure the vehicle fleet's firmware to transmit telemetry payloads directly via HTTP POST to a serverless Cloud Run function.
Transmit telemetry data via UDP directly into BigQuery utilizing the BigQuery Storage Write API.
Deploy a highly available Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance in each region to act as the primary MQTT listener and state store.
explanation: Following the deprecation of Cloud IoT Core, Google Cloud's official architectural guidance for IoT workloads is to deploy robust partner/third-party MQTT brokers (like EMQX, HiveMQ, or Litmus) on scalable infrastructure (GKE or VMs). These brokers handle the complex device authentication (X.509) and MQTT connection pooling, and then natively bridge the high-throughput message streams into Cloud Pub/Sub for downstream Google Cloud analytics.

category: Section 5: Managing implementation
question: You are implementing a hybrid cloud network via Dedicated Interconnect. You must seamlessly route DNS queries between your on-premises network and your Google Cloud VPC. The on-premises servers must resolve `*.gcp.internal` domains, while Compute Engine VMs must resolve `*.corp.local` domains hosted on your on-premises Active Directory. Which two Cloud DNS configurations are required? (Select 2)
options: Create a Cloud DNS Inbound Server Policy in the VPC.
Create a Cloud DNS Outbound Server Policy in the VPC.
Create a Cloud DNS Managed Private Zone for the `*.corp.local` domain.
Implement a Cloud NAT gateway to translate the internal DNS queries.
Configure an Organization Policy constraint enforcing DNSSEC globally.
explanation: To allow on-premises systems to resolve Google Cloud DNS names (`*.gcp.internal`), you must configure an Inbound Server Policy, which provides internal IP addresses that on-premises systems can query over the Interconnect. To allow Google Cloud VMs to resolve on-premises domains (`*.corp.local`), you must configure an Outbound Server Policy (DNS forwarding zone), which forwards queries originating in the VPC to your on-premises DNS servers.

category: Section 6: Ensuring solution and operations reliability
question: According to the Operational Excellence pillar of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework, organizations must define reliable Service Level Objectives (SLOs). You manage a critical microservice that processes user profile updates. To accurately measure the user experience, which metric represents the most appropriate Service Level Indicator (SLI) for the *latency* of this service?
options: The proportion of valid HTTP GET requests completed in under 200 milliseconds, measured at the external load balancer.
The average CPU utilization percentage of the GKE nodes hosting the microservice pods over a 24-hour period.
The total number of database transactions successfully committed per second to the backend Cloud SQL instance.
The amount of time the Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) team requires to deploy a new version of the microservice.
explanation: An SLI is a carefully defined quantitative measure of some aspect of the level of service that is provided. To reflect actual user experience, latency SLIs should measure the proportion of successful requests that fall within a defined time threshold (e.g., < 200ms) as observed as close to the user as possible (like the load balancer). Internal metrics like CPU or database TPS do not directly indicate if the user is experiencing slow load times.

category: Section 1: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture
question: A life sciences company is designing a high-performance computing (HPC) workload on Compute Engine to sequence genomes. The workload consists of tightly coupled MPI (Message Passing Interface) nodes that constantly exchange data and require absolute sub-microsecond network latency. They want to ensure the VMs are physically located as close together as possible on the Google network infrastructure. Which placement policy should they utilize?
options: Deploy the instances using a Spread placement policy.
Deploy the instances using a Compact placement policy.
Provision the instances within a Multi-zone managed instance group.
Provision a Regional managed instance group utilizing preemptible VMs.
explanation: A Compact placement policy specifies that Compute Engine should locate the virtual machines as close to each other as physically possible (within the same zone). This minimizes network latency and maximizes bandwidth between the instances, which is a strict requirement for tightly coupled HPC and MPI workloads. A Spread policy does the exact opposite, physically separating nodes for high availability.

category: Section 2: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure
question: You are provisioning the foundational infrastructure for a new financial application. The security team mandates that the Compute Engine application instances must *not* possess external public IP addresses. However, these private instances must still be able to securely download OS patches from the public internet, and pull private container images hosted in Google Cloud Artifact Registry. Which two network services must you provision to enable this connectivity? (Select 2)
options: Cloud NAT.
Private Google Access.
Cloud VPN.
Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) TCP forwarding.
VPC Network Peering.
explanation: Cloud NAT (Network Address Translation) provides outbound-only internet access for instances without external IP addresses, allowing them to download necessary OS patches from external public repositories. Private Google Access allows instances on a subnet without external IP addresses to securely reach Google APIs and services (such as Artifact Registry or Cloud Storage) using Google's internal private network.

category: Section 6: Ensuring solution and operations reliability
question: Following the Reliability pillar's guidance on designing for graceful degradation, your global web application relies on an advanced, AI-powered recommendation engine hosted on Vertex AI. During a regional outage, the recommendation engine API times out. What architectural pattern should your web frontend implement to optimize the user process and maintain reliability?
options: Retry the connection to the recommendation engine indefinitely using exponential backoff to ensure accurate data.
Return a hard HTTP 500 error to the client browser and prompt the user to manually reload the page in 5 minutes.
Catch the API timeout and automatically serve a pre-calculated, fallback list of globally popular items to the user.
Redirect the user to a static 'System Under Maintenance' page hosted on a secondary Cloud Storage bucket.
explanation: Graceful degradation dictates that when a non-critical dependency (like personalized recommendations) fails, the core system should continue to function in a diminished but useful capacity. By catching the timeout and serving a fallback list (like 'Top 10 Global Sellers'), the user can continue their shopping journey seamlessly, maintaining high availability for the core business transaction. Retrying indefinitely or showing 500 errors ruins the user experience.

category: Section 5: Managing implementation
question: A data engineering team is implementing a massive BigQuery data warehouse. They need to share specific subsets of a highly confidential sales table (e.g., exposing only rows where `region = 'EMEA'`) to the European sales department. They must accomplish this without duplicating data, creating separate tables, or managing complex intermediary views. How should they manage this data implementation?
options: Create an Authorized View containing a `WHERE region = 'EMEA'` clause, and grant the European team access solely to the view.
Utilize BigQuery column-level security and use policy tags to restrict the `region` column.
Export the EMEA data to a CSV file in a regional Cloud Storage bucket and grant the team IAM Storage Object Viewer.
Implement BigQuery row-level security by defining row-level access policies (RLS) directly on the primary table.
explanation: Row-level security (RLS) allows you to define policies directly on the source table that restrict which rows are returned to a user based on their identity or group membership. This is the modern, Google-recommended approach for enforcing fine-grained row access without data duplication or the management overhead of maintaining dozens of Authorized Views.

category: Section 3: Designing for security and compliance
question: A healthcare enterprise is designing for strict compliance. They utilize the Cloud Healthcare API to store sensitive patient HL7v2 messages. A primary regulatory requirement mandates that any administrative access to this environment by Google Cloud support personnel must be explicitly authorized by the enterprise's security team in real-time, and cryptographically logged. Which security feature satisfies this requirement?
options: Key Access Justifications (KAJ).
Access Approval.
Cloud Audit Logs configured for Data Access.
VPC Service Controls.
explanation: Access Approval is a premium security feature that guarantees Google Cloud administrators and support personnel require explicit, cryptographic approval from the customer before they can access customer data or configurations. This directly addresses stringent compliance and sovereignty requirements regarding vendor access to sensitive workloads.

category: Section 1: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture
question: You are designing the architecture for a global, high-traffic SaaS application. The frontend consists of static assets (HTML/CSS/JS) and highly dynamic API endpoints. You want to serve the static assets from Google's global edge locations to minimize user latency, while routing dynamic API requests to a serverless backend running on Cloud Run. Which two networking components must you deploy to achieve this architecture? (Select 2)
options: Global External Application Load Balancer.
Internal TCP/UDP Network Load Balancer.
Cloud CDN.
Cloud VPN.
Cloud Router.
explanation: A Global External Application Load Balancer provides a single global Anycast IP address and utilizes URL maps to intelligently route traffic (e.g., routing `/api` traffic to Cloud Run via Serverless NEGs, and `/static` traffic to a backend bucket). Enabling Cloud CDN on the load balancer caches the static assets directly at Google's global edge points of presence (PoPs), vastly minimizing latency for the end user.

category: Section 2: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure
question: A startup is provisioning their foundational Google Cloud environment. To comply with best practices before provisioning any compute resources, they must establish a central, hierarchical structure for billing, centralized identity, and organization-wide security policies. Which two core components must they provision to establish this root architecture? (Select 2)
options: A Google Workspace or Cloud Identity account.
A Cloud Billing Account.
A Shared VPC network.
A GKE Autopilot Cluster.
A Cloud Key Management Service (KMS) Key Ring.
explanation: To establish the root node of the Google Cloud resource hierarchy (the Organization resource), you must first have a Google Workspace or Cloud Identity account, which provides the identity domain required to tie the Organization resource to your company. Secondly, you must establish a Cloud Billing Account to process payments and link projects so that cloud resources can be provisioned and financially tracked.

category: Section 1: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture
question: Case Study: Altostrat Media. Altostrat Media is planning to migrate their legacy on-premises MySQL database, which stores critical metadata for their entire content library. The database requires 99.99% availability, automated failover without application changes, and the ability to handle heavy read traffic for content discovery reporting without impacting the primary writer. Which Google Cloud architecture best satisfies these technical and business requirements?
options: Deploy Cloud SQL for MySQL with Regional High Availability (HA) enabled for automated failover, and provision a separate Read Replica to offload reporting queries.
Migrate the database to a multi-region Cloud Spanner instance using the MySQL dialect to ensure global consistency and infinite read scalability.
Deploy a MySQL cluster on Compute Engine instances across multiple zones, using persistent disks and a custom Pacemaker/Corosync configuration for failover.
Migrate the data to BigQuery to handle the heavy read traffic natively, and use BigQuery ML to predict database failover events.
explanation: Cloud SQL for MySQL with Regional High Availability (HA) synchronously replicates data to a standby zone, guaranteeing 99.99% availability and automated failover without requiring application code changes. Provisioning a separate Read Replica perfectly fulfills the requirement to offload heavy read/reporting traffic from the primary instance. Cloud Spanner is overkill and requires significant refactoring, while self-managing a Compute Engine MySQL cluster drastically increases operational overhead.

category: Section 2: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure
question: Case Study: Cymbal Retail. Cymbal Retail wants to modernize its technical stack by migrating its product catalog from an on-premises MongoDB database to a fully managed Google Cloud NoSQL document database. They need high availability, automatic horizontal scaling, and the ability to synchronize product updates in real-time to their mobile application, even when mobile users briefly lose network connectivity. Which two services and configurations should they provision? (Select 2)
options: Provision a Firestore database in Native mode.
Provision a Cloud Bigtable cluster with multi-cluster routing.
Enable offline data persistence within the mobile client application using the native Firebase SDK.
Use Datastream to replicate changes directly from the on-premises MongoDB to Memorystore for Redis.
Migrate the schema to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL and utilize the JSONB data type.
explanation: Firestore in Native mode is Google Cloud's fully managed, highly scalable NoSQL document database, making it the direct modernization path for a MongoDB document catalog. Crucially, the Firestore/Firebase client SDK natively supports offline data persistence, caching data locally on the mobile device and automatically synchronizing changes in real-time when network connectivity is restored, perfectly meeting Cymbal's mobile requirements.

category: Section 3: Designing for security and compliance
question: Following the 'Implement preemptive cyber defense' principle of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework, your enterprise wants to enforce a strict security posture. You must ensure that external public IP addresses can *never* be assigned to Compute Engine virtual machines anywhere within your Google Cloud organization, regardless of the IAM roles a developer holds. What is the most robust way to implement this guardrail?
options: Remove the `roles/compute.networkAdmin` role from all developers globally.
Create a VPC Firewall rule with priority 100 that explicitly denies egress traffic to `0.0.0.0/0`.
Apply the `compute.vmExternalIpAccess` Organization Policy constraint at the organization root, setting it to Deny All.
Implement a VPC Service Controls perimeter around the Compute Engine API.
explanation: Organization Policies provide centralized, programmatic control over your cloud resources. By enforcing the `compute.vmExternalIpAccess` constraint at the organization root, you establish an immutable, preventive guardrail that physically blocks the creation of external IPs on VMs across all projects and folders, superseding any elevated IAM permissions a user might possess.

category: Section 4: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes
question: Following the Cost Optimization pillar of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework, your finance team wants to implement a process to accurately allocate cloud costs back to specific business units (Marketing, HR, R&D). These units share resources across dozens of central Google Cloud projects. What is the most effective automated process to track and analyze these granular costs?
options: Create separate billing accounts for each business unit and require developers to manually link resources.
Enforce mandatory resource labels (e.g., `business_unit: hr`) using Organization Policies, export Cloud Billing data to BigQuery, and use Looker Studio for granular analysis.
Parse Cloud Audit Logs using Cloud Dataflow to detect resource creation events and calculate costs based on the user's email domain.
Use the Google Cloud Console Folders structure to aggregate costs, ensuring each business unit only deploys in their designated folder.
explanation: Resource labeling is the foundational best practice for granular cost allocation in shared project environments. By enforcing labels via Organization Policies, you guarantee compliance. Exporting the detailed Cloud Billing data to BigQuery allows finance teams to seamlessly slice, query, and visualize exact costs per label (business unit) using tools like Looker Studio.

category: Section 5: Managing implementation
question: You are migrating a legacy monolithic application to Cloud Run. The application is hardcoded to read a sensitive database password from a local file path at `/config/db_password.txt` upon startup. You want to manage this credential securely using Google Cloud native services without refactoring the application's file-reading source code. How should you implement this?
options: Store the password in Secret Manager and configure the Cloud Run service to mount the secret as a volume at `/config/db_password.txt`.
Bake the password directly into the container image using a multi-stage Docker build, ensuring the file lands at `/config/db_password.txt`.
Create a custom Docker entrypoint script that downloads the password from a private Cloud Storage bucket and writes it to the local file path before starting the app.
Store the password in a Cloud SQL table and use a Cloud Run function to inject it into the container at runtime.
explanation: Cloud Run natively integrates with Secret Manager. You can configure a Cloud Run service to expose a secret as a volume mount. The secret is securely injected into the container's in-memory filesystem at the specified path (`/config/db_password.txt`) during startup. This perfectly satisfies the requirement to securely manage the secret without modifying the legacy application's source code.

category: Section 6: Ensuring solution and operations reliability
question: Following the Reliability pillar's 'Design for graceful degradation' principle, your global e-commerce application uses a microservices architecture on GKE. The critical Checkout service relies on a third-party legacy Payment API that frequently experiences severe latency spikes and timeouts. Which two architectural patterns should you implement to prevent the payment gateway's degradation from exhausting threads and causing cascading failures across your entire application? (Select 2)
options: Implement the Circuit Breaker pattern using Cloud Service Mesh to detect timeouts and fast-fail requests to the payment gateway when it is unhealthy.
Configure synchronous retries on the frontend with a fixed 1-second delay to ensure payment requests eventually succeed.
Utilize Cloud Tasks or Pub/Sub to decouple the payment processing, queueing the requests asynchronously if immediate consistency is not strictly required.
Increase the TCP connection timeout threshold on the external Application Load Balancer to 10 minutes to wait for the legacy API.
Configure the GKE Cluster Autoscaler to aggressively add more nodes whenever the Checkout service experiences high latency.
explanation: The Circuit Breaker pattern (easily implemented via Cloud Service Mesh) detects when a downstream service is struggling and deliberately fast-fails incoming requests to protect upstream services from thread exhaustion. Decoupling the architecture using asynchronous message queues (Cloud Tasks/Pub/Sub) absorbs the shock of upstream latency, allowing the frontend to remain responsive while the backend processes the payments at its own pace. Increasing timeouts or retrying synchronously makes cascading failures much worse.

category: Section 1: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture
question: Case Study: EHR Healthcare. EHR Healthcare wants to extract insights from legacy HL7v2 medical records stored on-premises to predict industry trends. They need a scalable architecture to securely ingest these records, transform them into a modern interoperable format, and analyze them using machine learning, while maintaining strict HIPAA compliance. Which architecture should you recommend?
options: Upload the HL7v2 flat files to Cloud Storage, and use the Cloud Vision API to OCR the text into BigQuery for analysis.
Use the Cloud Healthcare API to securely ingest and automatically transform the HL7v2 messages into FHIR format, stream them to BigQuery, and build predictive models using BigQuery ML.
Use Datastream to synchronize the legacy records directly to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL, and run custom Python machine learning models on a dedicated Compute Engine VM.
Run an Apache Spark cluster on Dataproc to parse the HL7v2 messages using regex, store the results in Cloud Bigtable, and export to Looker for manual trend prediction.
explanation: The Cloud Healthcare API is a purpose-built, HIPAA-compliant service that seamlessly ingests legacy healthcare data (like HL7v2) and transforms it into the modern FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) standard. It natively integrates with BigQuery, allowing EHR to seamlessly stream the standardized data into a data warehouse where BigQuery ML can securely execute predictive machine learning models.

category: Section 2: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure
question: A digital media agency wants to automate their video transcoding pipeline. As soon as a raw video file is uploaded to a Cloud Storage bucket, a transcoding process must execute. The process relies on custom C++ binaries, takes up to 4 hours per video, and needs to scale massively without manual server provisioning. How should you provision this event-driven infrastructure?
options: Configure a Cloud Storage trigger to invoke a Cloud Run function (2nd gen) that executes the C++ binaries.
Use Eventarc to route the Cloud Storage object creation event to a Cloud Run service configured for maximum execution time.
Configure Eventarc to trigger a Cloud Run execution that invokes a Cloud Run Job or Google Cloud Batch job to process the video.
Schedule a Cloud Scheduler job to poll the Cloud Storage bucket every 5 minutes and spin up a Compute Engine instance for each file.
explanation: Video transcoding is a long-running, batch-oriented task. Cloud Run functions and Cloud Run services have strict maximum execution timeouts (usually up to 60 minutes). Because the job takes 4 hours, you must use Cloud Run Jobs or Google Cloud Batch, which are designed for serverless, long-running, run-to-completion tasks. Using Eventarc to trigger an orchestrator (like Workflows or a small Cloud Run service) that kicks off a Cloud Run Job perfectly satisfies the event-driven, scalable, long-running requirements.

category: Section 3: Designing for security and compliance
question: Case Study: KnightMotives Automotive. KnightMotives is processing highly sensitive autonomous vehicle telemetry data. They must ensure that the BigQuery datasets containing this data are completely isolated from the public internet to prevent data exfiltration. Furthermore, only authorized containerized workloads running in a specific on-premises GKE Enterprise cluster should be able to query this data. Which two security configurations must they implement? (Select 2)
options: Implement VPC Service Controls to create a secure perimeter around the BigQuery API, disabling access from external networks.
Assign the `roles/bigquery.dataViewer` IAM role directly to the GKE nodes' default Compute Engine service account.
Configure Workload Identity Federation on the GKE cluster, binding the specific Kubernetes service account to a dedicated Google Cloud service account with BigQuery permissions.
Use Google Cloud Armor to create a WAF rule that drops BigQuery ingress traffic originating from outside the corporate IP range.
Enable Default Google-Managed Encryption Keys on the BigQuery dataset to isolate the storage blocks.
explanation: VPC Service Controls mitigates data exfiltration by placing a logical security perimeter around Google Cloud APIs (like BigQuery), strictly blocking requests from outside the trusted boundary (the public internet). To allow the on-premises GKE pods to authenticate securely without hardcoding JSON keys, Workload Identity Federation must be used to map the external Kubernetes identity to a least-privilege Google Cloud IAM service account.

category: Section 4: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes
question: According to the Operational Excellence pillar of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework, organizations must conduct thorough post-incident reviews (postmortems). A severe production outage occurred when a new developer manually deleted a critical Cloud SQL instance via the Cloud Console. What should be the primary focus and outcome of the resulting postmortem?
options: Identify the developer responsible, document their mistake in their performance review, and mandate additional training.
Identify the systemic gaps that allowed a developer to manually delete a production database, and implement technical guardrails like IAM least privilege and database deletion protection.
Restore the database immediately from the latest automated backup, verify data integrity, and close the incident report.
Draft a new organizational policy requiring manual VP approval via email for all future database operations.
explanation: A core tenet of SRE and operational excellence is the *blameless* postmortem. The assumption is that human error is a symptom of a systemic problem. The postmortem must focus on the system: why did the developer have permissions to delete production data? Why wasn't 'deletion protection' enabled on the Cloud SQL instance? Fixing the systemic gaps ensures the incident cannot be repeated, whereas punishing the developer does not fix the vulnerable system.

category: Section 5: Managing implementation
question: You are managing the deployment of a new version of a critical microservice on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE). You want to route exactly 10% of user traffic to the new version (canary) to monitor error rates and latency before proceeding with a full 100% rollout. You want to accomplish this natively using Google Cloud's managed service mesh without manual load balancer configuration. Which resource should you configure?
options: Configure traffic splitting weights within a Cloud Service Mesh `TrafficRoute` or `VirtualService` resource.
Create a standard Kubernetes `Ingress` resource with custom Nginx annotations to distribute traffic.
Deploy a Kubernetes `Deployment` with a replica ratio of 9 old pods to 1 new pod.
Configure a Cloud Load Balancing backend bucket with an advanced URL map.
explanation: Cloud Service Mesh (based on Istio/Envoy) provides advanced, fine-grained traffic management capabilities. By defining a `VirtualService` or `TrafficRoute`, you can declaratively split traffic by exact percentages (e.g., 90% to v1, 10% to v2) independent of the number of underlying pods. Relying on replica ratios (9:1) is inexact, scales poorly, and is an anti-pattern when a service mesh is available.

category: Section 6: Ensuring solution and operations reliability
question: A high-traffic global retail website hosted on Compute Engine Managed Instance Groups behind a Global External Application Load Balancer is experiencing intermittent HTTP 502 (Bad Gateway) errors during a flash sale. The reliability team needs to diagnose the root cause quickly. Which two observability approaches should they take? (Select 2)
options: Examine Cloud Logging for the Load Balancer to analyze the HTTP status codes, backend health status, and latency metrics.
Enable VPC Flow Logs on the backend subnet to inspect the deep HTTP request headers being dropped.
Use Cloud Profiler to check the application's memory heap for immediate memory leaks.
Check Cloud Monitoring dashboards for CPU, memory, and disk I/O utilization on the backend Compute Engine instances.
Configure Cloud IDS (Intrusion Detection System) to inspect the payload contents for malicious SQL injections.
explanation: HTTP 502 Bad Gateway errors typically mean the load balancer cannot communicate with the backend instances (due to health check failures, timeouts, or backend crashes). Examining Cloud Logging for the load balancer provides immediate context on the connection failure. Cross-referencing this with Cloud Monitoring metrics (CPU/Memory) on the backend instances will quickly reveal if the instances are overwhelmed and crashing under the traffic spike. VPC Flow Logs do not inspect Layer 7 HTTP headers.

category: Section 1: Designing and planning a cloud solution architecture
question: An enterprise wants to migrate its on-premises Hadoop cluster to Google Cloud. They use Apache Spark extensively for nightly batch processing and want to completely minimize operational overhead and node management. Their legacy jobs rely on data stored in HDFS, which they plan to migrate to Cloud Storage. Which migration strategy is the most efficient and cost-effective?
options: Migrate the workloads to Dataproc Serverless, rewriting the job configurations to point to the Cloud Storage paths (`gs://`).
Provision a persistent Dataproc cluster and attach massive Persistent Disks to replicate the local HDFS storage.
Deploy a custom Hadoop cluster on Compute Engine instances using an Infrastructure as Code template.
Rewrite all legacy Apache Spark jobs into Apache Beam and execute them using Cloud Dataflow.
explanation: Dataproc Serverless lets you run Spark batch workloads without requiring you to provision and manage your own cluster. It automatically scales workload resources and you only pay for the time the job runs. By pointing the Spark jobs to Cloud Storage (`gs://`) instead of HDFS, you decouple compute from storage, fully aligning with Google Cloud big data best practices and minimizing operational overhead.

category: Section 2: Managing and provisioning a cloud solution infrastructure
question: Following the Performance Optimization pillar of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework, you are tasked with improving the performance of a global media portal serving static assets (images, CSS, JS). Users in Asia are experiencing high latency because the primary web servers are deployed in us-central1. What is the most effective architectural change to provision?
options: Provision a Global External Application Load Balancer in front of the instances and enable Cloud CDN.
Provision an Internal TCP/UDP Load Balancer and replicate the backend instances to the asia-east1 region.
Replicate the backend servers to all Google Cloud regions and use Cloud DNS geolocation routing policies to direct traffic.
Upgrade the us-central1 Compute Engine instances to Compute-optimized (C2) machine types with local SSDs.
explanation: For global distribution of static assets, enabling Cloud CDN on a Global External Application Load Balancer is the optimal architecture. Cloud CDN caches the static content at Google's edge points of presence (PoPs) closest to the user globally, drastically reducing latency for users in Asia without requiring the operational overhead of replicating backend servers to multiple regions.

category: Section 3: Designing for security and compliance
question: An enterprise mandates that all Google Cloud deployments must be continuously compliant with the CIS (Center for Internet Security) Google Cloud Foundations Benchmark. You need to implement a solution to continuously monitor compliance, detect misconfigurations (e.g., open firewalls, public buckets), and automatically trigger alerts to the security operations team. Which two services should you configure to achieve this? (Select 2)
options: Enable Security Command Center Premium/Enterprise to automatically scan for misconfigurations against industry benchmarks.
Configure Eventarc to listen for Security Command Center findings and trigger a Pub/Sub topic to alert the SecOps team.
Implement VPC Service Controls to actively scan and block non-compliant API calls.
Use Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) to enforce zero-trust access on the Google Cloud Console.
Enable Cloud Sensitive Data Protection (DLP) to scan all Persistent Disks for compliance violations.
explanation: Security Command Center Premium/Enterprise natively includes the Security Health Analytics service, which continuously monitors cloud resources for misconfigurations and maps them against compliance frameworks like the CIS Benchmark. To automatically alert the security team, you can export these findings in real-time by configuring Eventarc or native SCC exports to publish to a Pub/Sub topic, which can then trigger external ticketing or alerting systems.

category: Section 4: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes
question: Your data engineering team utilizes BigQuery to analyze petabytes of log data. You need to optimize the technical process to significantly reduce query costs and improve read performance. Analysts typically filter queries using the `event_timestamp` column (querying only the last 7 days) and aggregate the results by the `country_code` column. What is the best table optimization technique?
options: Partition the table by `event_timestamp` and cluster the table by `country_code`.
Partition the table by `country_code` and cluster the table by `event_timestamp`.
Store the raw log data in Cloud Storage and execute federated queries via BigQuery.
